464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented path from signup → first class → habit formation (risk: inconsistent experience, high early churn)
- **Churn & win-back**: no process for pause requests, cancellation triggers, or re-engagement (financial leakage)
- **Coaching standards & QA**: no Class Quality Audit or weekly coach task checklist (quality drift, accountability gaps)
- **Member retention mechanics**: no check-in cadence, milestone/goal tracking, or outcomes measurement (can't prove ROI, weak upsell/retention)
- **Referral programme**: no structure, incentives, or tracking (untapped organic growth)
- **Complaints & incidents**: no escalation or resolution SOP (compliance, member safety, reputational risk)
- **Data & GDPR**: no privacy, consent, or data handling process (legal exposure)
- **Supply chain**: no order/restock SOP (class disruption risk if equipment fails)
- **Financial controls**: likely gaps in billing disputes, refunds, package transfers (cash leakage)

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coaches (20/24)**: likely missing coaching development roadmap, 1:1 feedback cadence, progression criteria
- **Marketing (5/5)**: may lack monthly campaign workflow, referral promotion schedule, or win-back campaign triggers
- **Clients (23/33)**: 30-day goals, transformation pillars, and member outcomes workflows probably vague or missing
- **Facilities (5/6)**: emergency/incident response and deep clean schedules likely underdocumented

---

# **Build next**

1. **Member Onboarding (Day 1–Day 30)** — map signup → induction → first 5 classes → habit trigger; include coach intro, facility

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Arrest the churn spike immediately.** Attrition hit 8.33% in April (relocation spike — understandable), but June still sits at 5.74% and 0.00% churn suggests either data error or anomaly. Target: return to Nov–Dec levels (~1%). At 147 active clients, even 2% monthly loss = 3 members gone. Implement exit interviews, pause-membership offers, and loyalty rewards for 6+ month members. Revenue per member (AR/M) has compressed from £170 (Dec) to £120 (June) — this is your leak.

• **Price increase + tier upsell.** Current revenue £20.2k (May) dropped to £17.7k (June) despite 147 members (your highest). This signals either discount creep, low-tier mix, or class/service underutilisation. Audit the membership mix. Launch a "Premium Plus" tier (personal training slots, nutrition access, priority booking) at +£15–20/month. Target: convert 20% of base members within Q3. Revenue swing: £300–400/month.

• **Fill quiet classes systematically.** With a studio now 2x size, you've capacity for higher throughput. Review June class attendance patterns — identify 3 lowest-utilisation slots and either move/consolidate them or run a 4-week ambassador challenge (free month for 5 friends who sign up). Peak-time slots should hit 18–22 bodies minimum.

• **Introduce non-membership revenue lines.** Nutrition plans (£20–50/month subscription), merchandise (branded kit, 20% margin), or partner PT sessions (you take 30% commission). These sit outside headcount caps. Target: £500–800/month by end of Q3.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people costs are the crisis.** June non-people expenses: £9,390. Compare to May (£9,233) — flat. But look at the Q2 average (May–June): £9,312 vs Q1 average (Feb–Mar): £10,778. You've already cut ~£1,500/month post-relocation; that's good. **However**, Feb–Apr show wild swings (£8.2k → £10.9k → £15.6k → £30.5k relocation). Post-April stabilisation is real. Audit June in detail: utilities, rent (new studio), insurance, supplies. Are you paying premium rates on anything (lease, energy, cleaning)?

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower classes to JoJo** — JoJo already delivers 15 Build: Upper sessions; adding 17 Build: Lower shifts Grace's largest class load to your most capable coach and creates a coherent strength programme under one expert.

• **Redistribute Grace's PT (unknown volume) and No Sweat Intros to Eilis** — Eilis has capacity (48 sessions vs JoJo's 133) and is ready for introductory 1:1 work; this frees Grace entirely and gives Eilis higher-value delivery than the current mix.

• **Assign Grace's Elevate and Strongevity to Eilis** — Eilis already teaches Elevate (7 sessions); doubling her load here keeps class depth consistent and develops her coaching range without Tuesday pressure.

• **Stop treating Body Composition Scans as coaching load** — All 40 scans (20+9+11) currently consumed are admin, not skill-building. Rotate these quickly through whoever is on-site; this frees ~6–8 hours across the team for real coaching.

• **Audit JoJo's 133-session month** — At 44 PT, she is carrying the PT portfolio alone. Eilis's 11 PT should grow to 20–25 to prevent burnout and create resilience if JoJo is ill or on leave.

• **Cover Grace's Strongevity (3 sessions)** — Either fold into Eilis's Elevate slot or ask JoJo to pilot one session to test fit; this is your smallest gap and lowest priority.

**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a paid "Open Lift" block on Monday and Thursday evenings (6-7pm)** — these are your quietest days and likely have gaps between PT slots and evening classes. Position it as unstructured strength training for members who want flexibility, charge £5-8 per session, and it requires minimal staffing beyond supervision.

2. **Run a 30-min "Mobility + Coffee" workshop on Saturday mornings before peak hours** — Saturdays are quiet overall but a short, social mobility session (which ranks low) could draw members in earlier, leading to additional class attendance or retail spend.

**For quieter classes:**

3. **Move "Engine" to Wednesday at 6:15pm (right after Build: Upper if there's demand overflow)** — Engine is drastically underattended, and Wednesdays are your busiest day; members already in-gym for Build sessions may try a conditioning class if it's immediately available. Alternatively, rebrand it as "cardio finisher" (max 20 mins, drop-in friendly) to lower the intimidation factor and fit busy schedules.

4. **Create a "Barbelle + Elevate combo package"** — Barbelle has only 34 attendees in 90 days. Offer members who book Elevate a £3

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your coaches' reality, not theory**
Pull 2-3 of your best coaches aside for 30 mins each. Ask: "What do you *actually* sell members on?" vs. "What are you supposed to sell?" Their answer is your real menu. You'll spot gaps between what's listed and what moves the needle (e.g., maybe it's actually small-group strength training, not generic "classes").

**2. Price backwards from member willingness-to-pay, not your costs**
Run a quick Slack poll or clipboard survey: ask 10 recent joiners "What would you pay monthly for [specific thing]?" and "What would make you upgrade?" Your pricing anchors on what they'll actually fork over, not what feels fair to you. Then work out if your margin works.

**3. Build one-page checklists per service tier (not a fancy document)**
Coaches need it laminated or in Trello. Include: session length, what's covered (e.g., "form check + 3 programmed lifts + 1 mobility finisher"), what *isn't* (nutrition coaching? PT plan customization?), and exactly what the upgrade costs. Make it impossible to upsell vaguely.

**4. Create a "member-facing" vs. "coach-facing" split immediately**
Coach version = operational (what to deliver, time boundaries). Member version = benefits language ("Crush your strength goals" not "form correction"). Completely different documents. Test the member version on someone outside the gym—they should get it in 20 seconds.

**5. Lock it in with a 2-week trial run**

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

• You've cracked lead volume—July's 90 leads at £3/lead is a 4.5x improvement on June's £21/lead—but close rate has collapsed from 34% (Apr) to 8% (Jul). You're buying cheap leads that don't convert. The profit per sale has tanked from £66 (Apr) to £155 (Jul) despite lower cost/lead, because you're now spending £29 to acquire each paying customer instead of £22.

• Your May–June trajectory suggests you were optimising for lead quality (targeting, creative, messaging) but ran into budget constraints or audience saturation, so you've overcorrected into volume-at-all-costs. July's cheap leads smell like broad targeting, lookalike audience expansion, or creative that doesn't pre-qualify intent.

• The real problem isn't CPL—it's that you've swapped quality for quantity without converting it. 90 leads generating only 7 sales means 83 wasted leads and wasted follow-up time. Your sales team is drowning.

**Actions**

1. **Segment and audit the July lead source.** Pull the audience/placement/creative breakdown for July's 90 leads. Identify which cohort(s) produced the 7 sales—those are your true prospects. Kill or pause the placements/audiences/creatives driving high-volume, low-intent junk. You likely have 2–3 leaky channels inflating volume.

2. **Tighten targeting back to Apr–May intent signals.** Revert to Apr's audience specification (likely: women 25–45, Belfast metro, fitness/wellness interests, high purchase intent). Layer in exclusions for window-shoppers (people who clicked but didn't complete lead form, or clicked multiple times without converting). Test a smaller, hotter audience at higher CPL—you'll take 40 leads at £8 that close at 25% over 90 at £3 that close at 8%.

3. **Test pre-qualification creative.** July's bulk volume suggests generic "6-week trial" messaging. Create two ad variants: (A) generic trial offer, (B) specific pain-point (e.g.

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE — GROWING BUT VOLATILITY & MEMBER VALUE AT RISK

- **Rev/member declining 18% from peak (£138 in April → £113 in June).** Members aren't spending enough. Audit pricing tiers now: are platinum/off-peak/class packs being sold? At 139 members on £113/head you're leaving £3k+/month on the table vs April rate. Run upsell blitz on PT sessions, nutrition, and 6-month upfront discounts (better cash, lower churn).

- **Churn spiked to 11.9% in May, then dropped to 3.3% in June.** May was likely post-relocation noise. But *December's 11.8% and May's 11.9% are outliers* — investigate: onboarding drop after moves? Competitor activity? Push 90-day retention wins (check-ins at week 2 & 8); retention now beats new acquisition ROI at this stage.

- **Revenue is flat (last 3 months avg £15.5k vs prior 3 avg £15.4k) — no growth trajectory.** You've added 18 members since January (105→139) but revenue barely moved. Pricing hasn't kept pace with member growth. Raise base membership by £5–10/month effective July (grandfather current members if needed to manage churn); target £130+/member by August.

- **Quiet class fill rates unknown** — if shoulder hours (6–7am, 5–6pm) aren't 70%+ utilised, bundle off-peak memberships at £89/month (off-peak only) or offer unlimited classes as premium tier at £159. This captures price-sensitive members without cannibalising standard £119 tier.

---

## EXPENSES — STRUCTURAL ISSUE POST-RELOCATION

- **Cash out June (£20.6k) is 31% higher than revenue (£15.8k).** Excluding the April move, last 6 months show cash out averaging £19.1k vs revenue £15.7k — you're structurally overspending. Drill into June payroll, rent, utilities: is relocation rent now running £7–8k/month (vs old space)? If so, you *must* hit £20k+ revenue to break even. If not, a supplier or staffing cost is creeping. Pull a P&L; don't guess.

- **Staffing efficiency red flag:** 139 members on one studio with (assumed) 3–4 PT/instruct
